Milkshake Mania Wiki
Play
Wiki / Modding overview

Modding overview

New in v1.4: Milkshake Mania is moddable. Install community mods that add content or extend the game, entirely at runtime. There is no rebuild and no separate launcher. This page is the friendly tour; the Hook & registry reference is the exact contract, and the Example mods page has full working source.

Jump to: Learn the basics · Get started · Cookbook · Imports · Mod types · Hooks · Debugging · Safety · Building
Start here

Learn the basics first (5-minute read)

The fastest way to get lost in modding is to start by copy-pasting code you don't understand. This section explains the handful of ideas that make everything below make sense — read it once, and every example afterward will read as "oh, that's just X" instead of a magic incantation.

What a mod actually is

A mod is just a folder, zipped up. Inside, there's always a mod.json — a small file (the "manifest") that tells the game the mod's name, version, and what kind of mod it is. Beyond that, a mod is one of two things (or both):

  • Data — a registry.json file listing new content (flavors, countries, achievements…) as plain data. The game reads it and adds that content. No programming involved — it's the same idea as filling out a spreadsheet.
  • Code — a dist/main.js file that runs inside the game and can change how things behave (not just add static content). This is where hooks come in (explained below).

When you "install" a mod, nothing gets compiled or rebuilt — the game just reads your manifest, applies your data (if any), and runs your code (if any) inside a sandbox that hands it a specific, limited set of tools. It can't reach anything you didn't give it access to.

JSON, for absolute beginners

If you've never written JSON, here's everything you need: it's just keys and values, wrapped in curly braces. 

{
  "name": "Mango Tango",
  "multiplier": 3.0,
  "seasonal": null
}
  • "name": "Mango Tango" — a key (name) and its value ("Mango Tango"), joined by a colon. Text values always get double quotes; the key always does too.
  • "multiplier": 3.0 — numbers don't get quotes. "3.0" (with quotes) is text, not a number, and the game will reject it.
  • Every key/value pair except the last one in a { } or [ ] needs a comma after it. A trailing comma after the last one is the single most common JSON mistake — it'll make the whole file fail to parse.
  • [ ] square brackets mean a list — you'll see "flavors": [ {...}, {...} ], a list of flavor objects, even if you only add one.

If a mod won't load, 9 times out of 10 it's a JSON typo (a missing comma, quote, or brace). Paste your file into any free online "JSON validator" to find the exact spot — or just check the Mods panel, which shows the parse error.

Namespaces & ids — why your content gets a weird prefix

Every mod declares a namespace in its manifest — a short, unique-to-you slug (e.g. my_first_mod). Every piece of content you add gets automatically prefixed with it, so your "id": "mango_tango" flavor actually becomes my_first_mod:flavors/mango_tango inside the game. That's why: two different mods can both add a flavor called "cool" without colliding — they end up as two entirely different ids. You'll see these full ids referenced when one piece of content needs to point at another (e.g. a combo listing which flavors it needs).

Hooks — the one concept worth actually understanding

Almost everything a code mod does comes down to hooks, and the whole idea is simpler than it looks. Picture the moment the game computes your income: somewhere inside, a plain function runs, does some math, and returns a number. A hook lets your mod step into that exact moment. There are three ways to step in, and each answers a different question:

  • append — "let me see the answer, and hand back a different one." The game computes its normal value, then your function receives it and returns a replacement. This is how value * 1.25 works: the game says "the multiplier is 4", your mod says "I'll take that and make it 5" (4 * 1.25). Multiple mods can each append to the same hook — they just stack, one after another.
  • override — "don't bother computing it, I'll answer instead." Your function fully replaces the game's own logic. Powerful, but only one override can win per hook (the last one loaded), so use it sparingly — prefer append when a transform is all you need.
  • prepend — "just tell me when this happens, I'm not changing anything." Your function is called with the inputs right before the game's own logic runs, but its return value is ignored. Useful for reacting to something (logging, triggering your own effect) without touching the result.

Every hookable moment in the game has a stable hook id like "game/logic.ts@calcGlobalMultiplier" — read it as "the file this lives in, then which function." You don't need to memorize them: call gameApi.hooks() from inside your own mod's code to print the full, current list at runtime, with a one-line description of what each one does.

Quick glossary

  • Manifest — the mod.json file; every mod has exactly one.
  • Asset mod — data only (a registry.json), no code, no consent prompt.
  • Code mod — includes a dist/main.js that runs inside the game; needs the player's consent to enable.
  • Registry — one of the game's content lists (flavors, countries, achievements, …) that a mod can add entries to.
  • Hook id — the stable string name of one hookable moment, e.g. "game/logic.ts@calcGlobalMultiplier".
  • gameApi — read-only helpers and live data (formatting, registries, stats) your code can call.
  • register — the API a code mod calls to add content imperatively (the code-mod equivalent of writing a registry.json).

New to programming entirely? Start with Track A below (an asset mod — pure JSON, no code) and get comfortable with the manifest + one registry first. Code mods (Track B) are a natural second step once hooks click, and it's genuinely fine to stop at Track A forever — plenty of great mods are data-only.

Get started

Make your first mod in 5 minutes

You need three things: a text editor (Notepad works), a way to make a .zip (built into Windows and macOS), and the game. No coding, no installs, no build tools for a content mod. Follow whichever track fits you.

Track A — a content mod (no code). Adds a new flavor. Two text files, zipped.

  1. Make a folder called my_first_mod.
  2. Inside it, create mod.json and paste this:
{
  "manifestVersion": 1,
  "apiVersion": "1.4",
  "namespace": "my_first_mod",
  "displayName": "My First Mod",
  "description": "Adds a Mango Tango flavor.",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "asset",
  "authors": ["you"]
}

What each field is actually for — not just what to type:

  • manifestVersion — the manifest FORMAT version (not your mod's version). Always 1 today.
  • namespace — your mod's unique slug. Every id you add gets prefixed with it (see "Namespaces & ids" above) — pick something unlikely to collide with another mod.
  • displayName / description — what players see in the Mods panel. Purely cosmetic, but this is a player-facing listing, so make them clear.
  • version — your OWN mod's version (semver-ish, e.g. bump to 1.1.0 when you update it). Unrelated to apiVersion.
  • type — "asset" (data only, this example) or "code" (also runs a script — see Track B).
  • authors — a list (square brackets!) even for one name.
  1. Next to it, create registry.json and paste this:
{
  "flavors": [
    {
      "id": "mango_tango",
      "name": "Mango Tango",
      "multiplier": 3.0,
      "unlockCost": 120000,
      "color": "#f59e0b",
      "description": "Bright, juicy, a little tropical."
    }
  ]
}

Reading this: "flavors" is a list (square brackets) of flavor objects — you could add more by comma-separating additional { ... } blocks inside it. "id": "mango_tango" becomes my_first_mod:flavors/mango_tango once loaded (your namespace gets prefixed automatically — see "Namespaces & ids" above). multiplier is how much each blend of this flavor is worth relative to the base rate; unlockCost is its price in the Flavors tab. That's the whole shape — every other registry type (countries, combos, achievements, …) follows the same "a named list of objects with typed fields" pattern; see the reference for every field on every type.

  1. Zip the folder (right-click → Send to → Compressed / Zip). You want my_first_mod.zip containing the folder.
  2. In the game: main menu → Mods → Install → pick your zip → toggle it on → Reload.
  3. Open the Flavors tab — Mango Tango is there, tagged MOD. That's a published mod. 🎉

Track B — a code mod (basic JS). Changes how the game behaves. Still no build tools — just hand-write the JS.

  1. Make a folder my_code_mod with a mod.json — same as above but "type": "code" and add "code": { "entry": "dist/main.js" }.
  2. Inside it make a dist folder, and in there a main.js with:
// The game hands your mod these ready to use: hooks, gameApi, events,
// storage, ui, mods, register, crash. No imports needed.

// 1) +25% to all global income:
hooks.append("game/logic.ts@calcGlobalMultiplier", function (value) {
  return value * 1.25;
});

// 2) Add a button to the main menu that pops a message:
ui.addMenuButton({
  label: "Hi!",
  icon: "sparkles",
  onClick: function () {
    ui.notify("My code mod is running!", { tone: "success" });
  },
});

Walking through it: line 1 is an append hook (see "Hooks" above) on "game/logic.ts@calcGlobalMultiplier" — the moment the game computes your overall income multiplier. The game hands your function value (whatever it just calculated), and whatever you return replaces it — here, 25% more than whatever it would've been. Line 2 uses ui, one of the objects the game injects, to add a real menu button with a click handler; ui.notify(...) is a toast. Nothing here was imported — hooks and ui simply exist as globals inside your mod's code, already connected to the live game.

  1. Zip the folder, install it. Because it runs code, the panel asks you to tick consent first — then reload.
  2. You'll see the "Hi!" button on the menu and earn 25% more. Every hook id and API call is listed on the reference page — call gameApi.hooks() in your mod to print every hookable point at runtime.

If your first mod doesn't show up

  • Zip structure. The zip should contain the folder (e.g. my_first_mod/mod.json inside the zip), not just the loose files at the zip's root. If your zip tool put the files straight in, re-zip the parent folder.
  • Forgot to reload. Installing/enabling a mod stages it — nothing changes until you hit Reload.
  • JSON typo. The Mods panel shows the parse error and roughly where it is — a missing comma or quote is the usual culprit (see "JSON, for absolute beginners" above).
  • Code mod not running. Code mods need the consent checkbox ticked in the Mods panel before they'll execute — asset-only mods don't need this.
  • Still stuck? Open the browser/devtools console — a failed mod logs its error there too, in addition to the in-game warning.

Going further (optional). For TypeScript types, autocomplete, multi-file mods, and a real build command, grab the mod kit below. Pros: it's plain esbuild with @smm/modkit external — see Building. But you never need it; everything above ships as-is.

Cookbook

Recipes for the things people actually make

Each of these is a complete dist/main.js body — drop it in a code mod (see Track B) and it works. The game hands your mod hooks, gameApi, events, storage, ui, mods, register and crash as ready-to-use globals; no imports required.

1 · Change a number (hooks)

A hook lets you transform a value the game just computed. append takes the value and returns a new one. The id names the exact function — the reference lists all 30. 

// Double passive income, and make leveling 20% faster.
hooks.append("game/logic.ts@calcIncomePerSecond", function (v) {
  return v * 2;
});
hooks.append("game/leveling.ts@xpForLevelUp", function (xp) {
  return xp * 0.8;
});

2 · React to what the player does (events + storage)

events.on fires on blends, rebrands and save changes. storage remembers things across reloads, scoped to your mod and the current save. 

// Count every blend; celebrate every 100th.
var blends = storage.get("blends", 0);
events.on("blend", function (e) {
  blends += 1;
  storage.set("blends", blends);
  if (blends % 100 === 0) {
    ui.notify(blends + " blends and counting!", { tone: "success" });
  }
});

3 · Add real UI (ui)

ui gives you theme-matched buttons, modals, toasts and prompts — no HTML/CSS of your own needed. gameApi.stats() is a read-only snapshot of the live save. 

// A menu button that opens a little stats panel.
ui.addMenuButton({
  label: "Stats+",
  icon: "bar-chart-3",
  onClick: function () {
    var s = gameApi.stats();
    ui.modal({
      title: "My Stats",
      icon: "bar-chart-3",
      html: s
        ? "Lifetime money: " + gameApi.format.money(s.totalLifetimeMoney)
          + "
Blends: " + gameApi.format.number(s.totalMilkshakes) + ""
        : "Open a save first!",
    });
  },
});
// ui.icons() lists every valid icon name.

4 · Add content from code (register)

Everything a registry.json can do, the register API can do from code — handy when the values are computed. (For static content, plain registry.json is simpler and needs no consent.) 

register.registerFlavor("cosmic_cream", {
  name: "Cosmic Cream",
  multiplier: 4.2,
  unlockCost: 500000,
  color: "#8b5cf6",
  description: "Out of this world.",
});
// Also: registerCountry, registerCombo, registerShop,
// registerEmployee, registerUpgrade, registerLocation, registerBackground.

5 · Depend on (or avoid) another mod (mods + crash)

Check what else is installed. If a hard requirement is missing, stop cleanly with crash() — it disables your mod and shows the crash screen instead of failing halfway. 

if (!mods.has("blend_lib")) {
  crash("This mod needs the 'blend_lib' library mod installed.");
}
if (mods.has("hardcore_mode")) {
  ui.notify("Heads up: balance may feel odd with Hardcore Mode.", { tone: "warn" });
}
Bigger mods

Multiple files & imports

When one file gets unwieldy, split it. The loader understands both import/export and CommonJS require, and relative imports resolve to other files inside your zip — so you can write a mod the way you'd write any small JavaScript project, no bundler required. 

my_mod/
  mod.json
  dist/
    main.js       <- entry (mod.json's code.entry)
    balance.js    <- your own helper file
// dist/balance.js
export const INCOME_MULT = 1.5;
export function tax(amount) {
  return amount * 0.9;
}
// dist/main.js
import { INCOME_MULT, tax } from "./balance.js";
import { gameApi } from "@smm/modkit"; // optional: same as the global

hooks.append("game/logic.ts@calcGlobalMultiplier", function (v) {
  return v * INCOME_MULT;
});
hooks.append("game/logic.ts@calcManualBlendGain", function (v) {
  return tax(v);
});

The injected globals (hooks, ui, …) are available in every file, so importing from @smm/modkit is optional — it's mainly for TypeScript autocomplete. To share code across separate mods, publish a library mod and require() / import it by namespace — see the reference.

Basics

What a mod is

A mod is a .zip file containing a mod.json manifest and its content. You install it from the Mods button on the main menu. Mods are stored locally in your browser and applied when the game loads.

  • Every mod declares a namespace (lowercase letters, digits, underscores). All of its content is prefixed with it, e.g. my_mod:flavors/mango, so it can never clash with the base game (which uses smm:).
  • Mods apply on load. Enabling, disabling, or installing prompts a reload, not a rebuild.
Two kinds

Asset mods vs code mods

TypeContainsRuns code?Use it for
Asset modA declarative registry.json (data only)NoAdding content: flavors, countries, shops, employees, upgrades, locations. Safe, no consent needed.
Code modEverything an asset mod has, plus a compiled dist/main.jsYes, with consentChanging behavior: scaling income, retuning the XP curve, adjusting prices, and more.
Library modCompiled code whose module.exports is shared via require("<namespace>")Yes, with consentShared helpers other mods depend on. Loaded before its dependents; declare it in a mod's dependencies.

A code mod is a superset of an asset mod: it can ship content and run code. An asset mod is the safe, data-only subset.

Code mods

Hooks, in plain terms

Code mods do not patch arbitrary internals (the shipped game is obfuscated). Instead the game exposes a fixed set of hook points - specific functions a mod may extend. A mod registers a handler against a hook by its string id:

  • append - runs after the function; transforms its return value. Multiple mods stack in load order.
  • prepend - runs before; used to observe or react to the inputs.
  • override - replaces the result entirely. Use sparingly; if two mods override the same hook the last one (alphabetically) wins and a warning is shown.

v1.4 wires 30 hook points — income, prestige, manual blend, special outcomes, customer & location ratings, the XP curve, level rewards, country / shop / upgrade / location pricing, research, the Flavor Lab, and generated reviews. The policy is "almost everything is hookable," and the set grows every version. The full list with signatures is on the reference page — or call gameApi.hooks() in your mod to print every hookable point at runtime.

Beyond hooks, code mods can also: react to lifecycle events (every blend, every rebrand), keep persistent per-mod storage across sessions, read the live flavor/country registries, check which other mods are installed (and crash() safely on a hard incompatibility), and even add their own buttons to the main menu. Together that's enough for reactive, stateful mods with real UI - not just number tweaks.

Testing

Debugging & when something breaks

You don't need a build pipeline to iterate — edit, re-zip, re-install, reload. A few things make it painless:

  • Open the browser console (F12). Your console.log shows up there, and any mod error is printed with a [mod…] prefix.
  • Discover the surface at runtime. gameApi.hooks() returns every hookable point ({ id, kind, desc }); ui.icons() lists valid icon names; mods.list() shows what's installed. console.table(gameApi.hooks()) is a nice way to browse.
  • If your mod throws — in a hook, an event handler, a button, or top-level code — the game shows a crash screen with a Reload button rather than a blank page, and tells you which mod was at fault. The current frame isn't corrupted; you get a clean reload. Call crash("why") yourself for a tidy, labelled stop.
  • A bad mod can't brick you. If a mod breaks the page on load, the game auto-disables it on the next boot. You can always disable or uninstall from the Mods panel and reload.
  • Your save is separate from mods. Turning a mod off (or having it crash) never touches your progress.

Sharing & updating. A mod is just its .zip — send it to anyone and they install it the same way. Shipping a new version? Bump version in mod.json, re-zip, and re-install over the old one (same namespace). Keep your namespace stable so saved content and per-mod storage carry over.

Safety

Trust, consent & warnings

You install your own mods, the same trust model as a browser extension. The Mods panel is explicit about risk:

  • A mod flagged unsafe by its author shows a red warning.
  • Any code mod is labelled as running code and changing gameplay, and will not execute its code until you tick the consent box.
  • Version and load-order conflicts are surfaced as warnings.

Only install mods from sources you trust. Code mods run their own JavaScript in your browser. The game's EULA covers user-made content.

Build your own

Making a mod

The mod kit is NOT required to install or play mods. Loading a mod is always just: Mods panel → Install → pick a zip → toggle it on → Reload. @smm/modkit is a separate, entirely optional download for people writing their own mods — it's encouraged if you're authoring one (TypeScript types, autocomplete, and a real build command make code mods much easier to write), but you can also hand-write plain JavaScript with zero tooling (see below) and skip it completely.

  1. Create a folder named after your mod with a mod.json (see the reference for the schema).
  2. Asset mod: add a registry.json with your content. No build step. Done.
  3. Code mod: write TypeScript against the @smm/modkit package (types + autocomplete), then transpile + bundle to dist/main.js with @smm/modkit kept external. The game does not compile TypeScript for you — you ship the compiled bundle.
  4. Zip the folder and install it via the Mods panel.

Installing the mod kit (for authoring — skip this if you're just playing mods)

  1. Click Download @smm/modkit below and unzip it anywhere on your machine.
  2. Create your mod's folder next to the unzipped build.mjs (e.g. as a sibling folder) — that's how the build script finds it.
  3. Write your code mod's TypeScript against @smm/modkit's types (import { hooks, gameApi, ui, ... } from "@smm/modkit" — full autocomplete in any editor that understands the bundled tsconfig.json).
  4. Run node build.mjs (or node build.mjs your_mod_name for just one mod) — it transpiles + bundles your TypeScript to dist/main.js, keeping @smm/modkit external so the game can inject the real API at runtime.
  5. Zip your mod's folder (mod.json + dist/main.js + any registry.json/assets) and install it from the Mods panel — same as any other mod.

Get the mod kit — it's everything you need to build. The @smm/modkit download bundles the TypeScript declarations (type-checking + autocomplete), build.mjs — the actual build script (esbuild: --bundle --format=cjs --external:@smm/modkit) — and a ready tsconfig.json. Unzip, drop your mod folder next to build.mjs, run node build.mjs, zip, install. The bundled README walks through it.

Download @smm/modkit Just build.mjs

No build tools? Hand-write it. The build step is optional — it exists for TypeScript types, autocomplete and multi-file mods. What compilation produces is just a plain JavaScript file, and you can write that directly. A complete hand-written code mod is two files: 

my_mod/
  mod.json       <- manifest
  dist/main.js   <- plain JS, written by hand (no imports, no build)
// dist/main.js — the game injects the API as globals when your mod runs:
// hooks, gameApi, events, storage, ui, mods, crash, register (+ require)
hooks.append("game/logic.ts@calcGlobalMultiplier", function (v) {
  return v * 1.25; // +25% global income
});
ui.addMenuButton({
  label: "Hi",
  icon: "sparkles",
  onClick: function () {
    ui.notify("Hello from my hand-written mod!", { tone: "success" });
  },
});

Zip the folder, install it from the Mods panel — that's a working mod. This is also exactly what a compiled mod looks like inside: build.mjs just turns your TypeScript into a file like the one above (CommonJS, with @smm/modkit imports left as require("@smm/modkit") calls the game resolves at runtime). Peek at any example mod's dist/main.js to see real compiled output.

Example mods show the API in practice: asset mods (Neapolitan Pack, Flavors+), small and large code mods (Lucky Blends, Tycoon Mode), and stateful companion mods (Shake Journal, Journal+) that show events, storage, and UI injection working together. Their dist/main.js bundles are genuinely compiled from their src/ by the kit's build script — read the source to see each API used in context.
